title: The Tarot Card Deck Created by Salvador Dalцґ
description:
in Art |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
The Tarot has long been a tool of charбґlaбґtans. But it has also long been embraced by brilбґliant,
body:
in Art |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
The Tarot has long been a tool of charбґlaбґtans. But it has also long been embraced by brilбґliant, unconбґvenбґtionбґal thinkers, many of whom themбґselves have a touch of the charбґlaбґtan about them (and who would just as likeбґly admit it with a smile). William Butбґler Yeats was a fan, as is visionбґary Chilean filmбґmakбґer, artist, writer, and psyбґchoбґnaut Aleбґjanбґdro Jodorбґowsky, who has recordбґed his own Youtube series explainбґing his take on this clasбґsic mode of divбґinaбґtion. With its archeбґtypбґal symбґbolбґism, the TarotБ─≥s appeal to artists should be obviбґous. Most of them, like Jodorбґowsky, find farб═more interбґestбґing uses for it than forбґtune-telling. Б─°You must not talk about the future,Б─² Jodorбґowsky tells us in his series, Б─°the future is a con. The tarot is a lanбґguage that talks about the present.Б─²
What might anothбґer visionбґary artist, Salбґvador Dalцґ, think of JodorowskyБ─≥s Tarot interбґpreбґtaбґtions? WeБ─≥ll nevбґer know, but I susбґpect he would find them enchantбґiбґng. Not only do the two seem like kinбґdred spirбґits, but Dalцґ devotбґed some part of his life to the Tarot, designбґing his own deck in the 70s.
Iniбґtialбґly, the project arrived as a comбґmisбґsion from proбґducбґer Albert Brocбґcoli for the James Bond film Live and Let Die. Б─°Likeбґly inspired by his wife Gala, who nurбґtured his interбґest in mysбґtiбґcism,Б─² writes ChicagoБ─≥s Museбґum of Conбґtemбґpoбґrary Art, Б─°Dalцґ eagerбґly got to work, and conбґtinбґued the project of his own accord whenб═the conбґtracбґtuбґal deal fell through.Б─²
It was just around this time that the Tarot saw a masбґsive resurбґgence in popбґuбґlarбґiбґty. The occult interбґests of the 60s counбґterбґculбґture were mainбґstreamed in the 70s thanks to books like Stuбґart KaplanБ─≥s Tarot Cards for Fun and Forбґtune Telling. But while Dalцґ had chanбґneled the vivid psyбґcheбґdelia of the age in an earбґliбґer illusбґtraбґtion project,б═1969Б─≥sб═Alice and Wonбґderбґland, his Tarot deck, writes Lisa Rainбґwaбґter at Galo magбґaбґzine, Б─°actuбґalбґly shows reserve. Yes, reserveБ─■as if his revбґerбґence for the tarot nearбґly humбґbles him.Б─² His knack for Б─°fanatбґiбґcal self-proбґmoбґtionБ─² does get the betбґter of him evenбґtuбґalбґly: he choosбґes his own face to repбґreбґsent the Magiбґcian (above).


	

Overбґall, the deck comбґbines the eclecбґtic oriбґgins of occult pracбґtices with DalцґБ─≥s own unmisбґtakбґable senбґsiбґbilбґiбґty. DalцґБ─≥s Tarot is Б─°a pasбґtiche of old-world art, surбґreбґalбґism, kitsch, Chrisбґtбґian iconogбґraбґphy and Greek and Roman sculpбґture. Many of his recurбґring motifs such as the rose, the fly and the bullБ─≥s head are found throughбґout the deck.Б─² First pubбґlished in a limбґitбґed ediбґtion in 1984Б─■and reisбґsued since in ediбґtions by TASCHEN and in book form by othбґer pubбґlishбґersБ─■the deck includбґed an introбґducбґtoбґry bookбґlet that reads, in Spanбґish, Engбґlish, and French:
The Wizбґard (Arcanum I), Salбґvador Dalцґ, has transбґformed with his excepбґtionбґal art and his marбґvelous talбґent the 78 goldбґen plates of Б─≤The fabбґuбґlous book of ThotБ─≥ into as many artisбґtic marбґvels, each one of them duly signed by the hand of this unmatchбґable, interбґnalбґly famous painter Б─і such an extraбґorбґdiбґnary artisбґtic creбґation does not detract, in any way, from the TarotБ─≥s close symбґbolбґism. On the conбґtrary, it enhances with its capбґtiбґvatбґing beauбґty, the TarotБ─≥s esoбґteric and plasбґtic meanбґing.
See a preбґview video of the full Dalцґ deck above, purбґchase a limбґitбґed ediбґtion set here, or a much more affordбґable verбґsion here.
Note: An earбґliбґer verбґsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
Relatбґed Conбґtent:
Aleбґjanбґdro Jodorбґowsky Explains How Tarot Cards Can Give You Creбґative Inspiбґraбґtion
The Fasбґciбґnatбґing Hisбґtoбґry of Tarot Card Decks: From the Renaisбґsance to the Modбґern Day
Meet the Forбґgotбґten Female Artist Behind the WorldБ─≥s Most Popбґuбґlar Tarot Deck (1909)
Behold the Sola-Busбґca Tarot Deck, the Earбґliбґest Comбґplete Set of Tarot Cards (1490)
Carl Jung on the Powбґer of Tarot Cards: They Proбґvide Doorбґways to the Unconбґscious & Perбґhaps a Way to Preбґdict the Future
Josh Jones is a writer and musiбґcian based in Durham, NC.б═

title: The Forgotten Moment When Superman Fought Prejudice in America Instead of Villains (1950)
description:
in Comics/Cartoons, Education, Life |   	May 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
It makes sense that Superбґman would take a tolбґerбґant view of immiбґgr
body:
in Comics/Cartoons, Education, Life |   	May 15th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
It makes sense that Superбґman would take a tolбґerбґant view of immiбґgrants and othбґer minoriбґties, givбґen that he himбґself arrived on Earth as a refugee from the planбґet Krypбґton.
The Man of Steel may strike you as an unlikeбґly mouthбґpiece for proбґgresбґsive ideals, but 1950 found him on a book covбґer,б═above, engaged in conбґverбґsaбґtion with a small crowd of mostбґly white boys:
Б─°Б─іand rememбґber, boys and girls, your school Б─⌠ like our counбґtry Б─⌠ is made up of Amerбґiбґcans of many difбґferбґent races, reliбґgions and nationбґal oriбґgins, so Б─і If YOU hear anyбґbody talk against a schoolбґmate or anyбґone else because of his reliбґgion, race or nationбґal oriбґgin Б─⌠ donБ─≥t wait: tell him THAT KIND OF TALK IS UN-AMERICAN. HELP KEEP YOUR SCHOOL ALL-AMERICAN!Б─²
In othбґer words, citбґiбґzens must steel themбґselves to take action, because you canБ─≥t always count on a superбґhero to show up and make things right.
The cheap paper jackбґet, above, was disбґtribбґuted to school chilбґdren by the Instiбґtute For Amerбґiбґcan Democбґraбґcy, an offбґshoot of the New York-based Anti-Defamaбґtion League.
Meanбґwhile, a full colбґor verбґsion of the 66-year-old illusбґtraбґtion has been makбґing the rounds on social media. Let us conбґsidбґer it a placeбґholdбґer. Evenбґtuбґalбґly someбґoneб═will sureбґly takeб═it back to the drawбґing board to add more girls, chilбґdren with disбґabilбґiбґties, and chilбґdren of colбґor.

Note: An earбґliбґer verбґsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
Relatбґed Conбґtent:
1950s Batбґman Carбґtoon Tells Kids: Б─°DonБ─≥t Believe Those Crackбґpot Lies About Peoбґple Who Worбґship DifбґferбґentбґlyБ─²
Read Marбґtin Luther King and The Montбґgomery Stoбґry: The Influбґenбґtial 1957 Civбґil Rights Comбґic Book
Dr. Seuss Draws Anti-Japanбґese Carбґtoons Durбґing WWII, Then Atones with Horбґton Hears a Who!
The Origбґiбґnal 1940s Superбґman Carбґtoon: Watch 17 Clasбґsic Episodes Free Online
Ayun Halбґlбґiбґday is an author, illusбґtraбґtor, theбґater makбґer in NYC.

title: The Most Influential Philosophers Explained in 26 Minutes: From Socrates to Wittgenstein
description:
in Philosophy |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The quesбґtion of who are the fifбґteen most influбґenбґtial philosoбґphers of all time may
body:
in Philosophy |   	May 14th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The quesбґtion of who are the fifбґteen most influбґenбґtial philosoбґphers of all time may not arise at every conбґverбґsaбґtion down at the pub Б─■ not outбґside the cirбґcle of Open Culбґture readбґers, in any case. But even among non-speбґcialбґists, it could spark a liveбґliбґer debate than you might imagбґine. Names likeб═Socrates,б═Arisбґtoбґtle,б═Descartes, andб═Marxб═are known, after all, even among the genбґerбґal pubбґlic whoБ─≥ve nevбґer read a page of philoбґsophбґiбґcal text. All of them appear inб═the milбґlion-viewed video fromб═Jayбґdone Hisбґtoбґryб═above, which takes its own crack at namбґing a top fifбґteen. Its 26 minбґutes also proбґvide a brief bioбґgraphбґiбґcal sketch of each one, inforбґmaбґtive if litбґtered with odd misбґproбґnunбґciбґaбґtions, plus a capбґsulized sense of these philosoбґphersБ─≥ lastбґing ideas.
In purбґsuit of truth,б═Socratesб═creбґatбґed the quesбґtionбґing method of diaбґlogue that bears his name.б═Plaбґto, SocratesБ─≥ stuбґdent, advoбґcatбґed for rule by the enlightбґened and the purбґsuit of knowlбґedge through the conбґtemбґplaбґtion of pure forms. Rejectбґing PlaбґtoБ─≥s method,б═Arisбґtoбґtleб═dedбґiбґcatбґed himбґself to sysбґtemбґatбґic empirбґiбґcal obserбґvaбґtion. On the othбґer side of the world,б═Conбґfuбґciusб═spread teachбґings about the culбґtiбґvaбґtion of moral virtue to mainбґtain the social relaбґtionбґships he saw as the basic buildбґing blocks of civбґiбґlizaбґtionбґal order, which Chiбґna evenбґtuбґalбґly adoptбґed as its state phiбґlosбґoбґphy. Back in Europe,б═Augusбґtineб═synбґtheбґsized Chrisбґtбґian theбґolбґoбґgy and clasбґsiбґcal phiбґlosбґoбґphy, layбґing the groundбґwork for medieval thought.б═Thomas Aquinas, too, dedбґiбґcatбґed himбґself to a comбґbiбґnaбґtion of faith and reaбґson, makбґing a suite of oft-citбґed arguбґments for the exisбґtence of God.
Seekбґing a founбґdaбґtion of absoluteбґly cerбґtain knowlбґedge,б═Renц╘ Descartesб═arrived at self-awareбґness, famousбґly declarбґing, Б─°I think, thereбґfore I amБ─² and articбґuбґlatбґing his eponyбґmous dualбґisбґtic worldбґview. Even apart from his work on the nature of knowlбґedge,б═John LockeБ─≥s thoughts on social orgaбґniбґzaбґtion and govбґernбґment live on in Enlightбґenбґment-influбґenced modбґern democбґraбґcies even today.б═David Humeб═mountбґed funбґdaбґmenбґtal chalбґlenges to estabбґlished ideas of empiriбґcism, quesбґtionбґing our very notion that future events will mirбґror past expeбґriбґence.б═Jean-Jacques Rousseauб═introбґduced the conбґcepбґtion of legitбґiбґmate politбґiбґcal authorбґiбґty as arisбґing from the Б─°genбґerбґal will,Б─² a social agreeбґment among free and equal indiбґvidбґuбґals, which turned out to be cenбґtral to the jusбґtiбґfiбґcaбґtions of the French Revбґoбґluбґtion.б═Immanuel Kantб═worked to bridge the gap between ratioбґnalбґism and empiriбґcism, recбґonбґcilбґing the role of both expeбґriбґence and the mindБ─≥s physбґiбґcal strucбґture to the forбґmaбґtion of knowlбґedge.
Among othбґer conбґcepts,б═Georg Wilбґhelm Friedrich Hegelб═defined that ofб═dasein, which encapбґsuбґlates the human mode of being (and which requires a lifeбґtime spent with his writбґings to grasp, if even then). A proбґfesбґsionбґal jourбґnalбґist and hisбґtoбґriбґan,б═Karl Marxб═described human hisбґtoбґry through ecoбґnomбґic strucбґtures and class strugбґgle, and his vision of a perбґfectбґly equal sociбґety ahead still remains comбґpelling to many.б═Friedrich Nietбґzscheб═declared that Б─°God is dead,Б─² placбґing the burбґden of definбґing moralбґiбґty on man, and specifбґiбґcalбґly a figбґure he called theб═ц°berбґmenбґsch.б═Ludбґwig Wittgenбґsteinб═took it upon himбґself to explain the relaбґtionбґship between lanбґguage and realбґiбґty with the highбґest rigбґor. In the comбґments, the videoБ─≥s creбґator teasбґes a part two, which makes one wonбґder which philosoбґphers will be includбґed:б═Spinбґoza?б═Heiбґdegбґger?б═Sartre? The year and a half itБ─≥s takбґen so far is sureбґly long enough for the narбґraбґtor to have learned to proбґnounce them.
Relatбґed conбґtent:
Free Online Phiбґlosбґoбґphy Coursбґes
Watch Aniбґmatбґed Introбґducбґtions to 35 Philosoбґphers by The School of Life: From Plaбґto to Kant and Fouбґcault
Bryan MageeБ─≥s In-Depth, Uncut TV Conбґverбґsaбґtions With Famous Philosoбґphers (1978Б─⌠87)
28 Imporбґtant Philosoбґphers List the Books That Influбґenced Them Most Durбґing Their Colбґlege Days
The Daiбґly Habits of Highбґly Proбґducбґtive Philosoбґphers: Nietбґzsche, Marx & Immanuel Kant
The 20 Most Influбґenбґtial Acaбґdбґeбґmбґic Books of All Time: No Spoilбґers
Based in Seoul,б═Colбґinб═Marshallб═writes and broadбґcasts on cities, lanбґguage, and culбґture. HeБ─≥s the author of the newsletбґterб═Books on Citiesб═as well as the booksб═М∙°Й╣ґ Л ■Л∙Ґ Й╦┬Лї─б═(No Sumбґmaбґrizбґing Korea) andб═Koreбґan Newtro.б═Folбґlow him on the social netбґwork forбґmerбґly known as Twitбґter atб═@colinmarshall.

title: At Joy Machine, 'Feel Free' Plumbs the Tension Between Chaos and Control
description: Joy Machine is pleased to present Feel Free, a group exhibition featuring new works by Rachel Hayden, Paulina Ho, Hanna Lee Joshi, and Jeremy Mira
body:
Joy Machine is pleased to present Feel Free, a group exhibition featuring new works by Rachel Hayden, Paulina Ho, Hanna Lee Joshi, and Jeremy Miranda. The opening reception will be held from 6 to 8 p.m. on May 15, 2026.
Attempting to create order and find clarity amid chaos is human instinct. Since time immemorial, weБ─≥ve endeavored to make sense of a world in which reason and certainty are never assured. Change, as the saying goes, is the only constant, which means notions of autonomy or control are a subjective fantasy rather than a concrete reality. In Feel Free, we witness four artists grappling with this enduring paradox. Each surrenders to the inevitability of change and focuses on the small instances of understanding that, for a brief moment, allow us to believe weБ─≥re closer to figuring it out.
Jeremy Miranda, Б─°Stock PotБ─² (2026), acrylic on panel, 16 x 20 inches
Hayden is known for her uncanny compositions that infuse flowers, plants, and insects with human emotion, often those that are unsightly or difficult to voice. SheБ─≥s described her work as a way Б─°to take controlБ─² amid situations that are so often out of our hands. For this exhibition, Hayden subsumes fruit and figures with the checkerboard pattern of a picnic blanket, utilizing color to make one indistinguishable from the other. As ants crawl along a characterБ─≥s face in the shape of perfectly arched brows, the artist gestures toward the brief intervals when disparate components align, creating an uncanny harmony.
Blending gouache and colored pencil into textured gradients, Joshi similarly reflects inarticulable experiences through her signature nude figures. In the bold Б─°Held like a flower,Б─² the artist presents an anonymous woman with a mass of black hair as she peers down at a single flower. The thin vines mimic the gestural qualities of her fingers, suggesting an affinity between the two.
Typically working in controlled acrylic on canvas, Ho shifts to textiles sourced from a thrift shop, rendering soft landscapes with Japanese indigo. This new direction emerged from an artist residency in Joseph, Oregon, following a trip to Andalusia, where she found inspiration in the cream-and-blue Moorish architecture. Frayed edges and bold gradients capture both movement and evolution, invoking a sense of the undone and the cyclical processes that often pattern our lives.
Rachel Hayden, Б─°Picnic BouquetБ─² (2026), acrylic on panel, 9 x 12 inches
Miranda, too, captures singular moments of impermanence. ThereБ─≥s a stock pot atop a roaring flame, a bundle of plump white asparagus bound by bands, and an antique ceramic sink unadorned by backsplash or countertop. To create his painterly compositions, Miranda incorporates a wet-sanding process that reveals how Б─°the painting has lived.Б─² Acknowledging that his own desires are not the sole factors in an artworkБ─≥s creation, he surrenders to the slippery qualities of memory and paint itself.б═
Feel Free is on view from May 15 to June 27, 2026. RSVP.
Paulina Ho, Б─°SpectacleБ─²(2026), Japanese indigo on fabric, 6.5 x 7 inches, 12 x 12 inches framed
Jeremy Miranda, Б─°FaucetБ─² (2026), acrylic on panel, 7.75 x 9.75 inches
Rachel Hayden, Б─°Self-Portrait as a Picnic BlanketБ─² (2026), acrylic on panel, 11 x 14 inches
Paulina Ho, Б─°RestfulБ─²(2026), Japanese indigo on fabric, 6.5 x 7 inches, 12 x 12 inches framed

title: Blood-Red Landscapes by Andrew McIntosh Conjure the Terrifying Unknown
description: Typically gravitating toward dreamy palettes of soft blues, grays, and oranges, Scottish artist Andrew McIntosh opts for a sanguine red in a new b
body:
Typically gravitating toward dreamy palettes of soft blues, grays, and oranges, Scottish artist Andrew McIntosh opts for a sanguine red in a new body of work. The crimson paintings continue McIntoshБ─≥s otherworldly landscapes that cast familiar forms like mountains and valleys in a strange, uncanny light. Glowing orbs float among the craggy terrain and veil the scenes in mystery.
Б─°These works sit somewhere between memory and inventionБ─■familiar landscapes interrupted by something I donБ─≥t fully understand,Б─² the artist says.
Б─°WhitneyБ─² (2026), oil on linen, 170 x 130 centimeters
On view at School Gallery, these bold pieces comprise the artistБ─≥s solo exhibition, I Hope This Transmission Finds You Soon. Evoking alien communication and the unknowns that surround us, even in recognizable spaces, the show draws on Cormac McCarthyБ─≥s 1985 novel Blood Meridian, a Gothic Western rife with violence and an unyielding desire for dominance. 
The gallery offers insight into the exhibition with an apt quote from the book:

The truth about the world Б─і is that anything is possible. Had you not seen it all from birth and thereby bled it of its strangeness it would appear to you for what it is, a hat trick in a medicine show, a fevered dream, a trance be populate with chimeras having neither analogue nor precedent, an itinerant carnival, a migratory tent show whose ultimate destination after many a pitch in many a mudded field is unspeakable and calamitous beyond reckoning.

I Hope This Transmission Finds You Soon is on view through May 30 in Folkestone, U.K. Find more from McIntosh on Instagram.
Б─°K2Б─² (2026), oil on linen, 38 x 43 centimeters
Б─°GasherbrumБ─² (2026), oil on linen, 38 x 43 centimeters
Б─°MatterhornБ─² (2026), oil on board, 20 x 15 centimeters
Detail of Б─°WhitneyБ─² (2026)

title: Nasher Museum's 'Everything Now All At Once' Celebrates Diversity, Resilience, and Joy
description: In Everything Now All At Once at the Nasher Museum of Art at Duke University, the title says it all. Dozens of works from the likes of  Nick Cave,
body:
In Everything Now All At Once at the Nasher Museum of Art at Duke University, the title says it all. Dozens of works from the likes of  Nick Cave, Ai Weiwei, Nina Chanel Abney, Wangechi Mutu, and many more represent a slice of the contemporary art world in which globalism and diversity are at the fore, and the lessons of the past inform how artists imagine the future.
Interestingly, the pieces are also decidedly analog, especially noteworthy as these worksБ─■alongside a few other multimedia and photographic additionsБ─■have been made throughout the era of light-speed technological advances. Painting and sculpture, in particular, have long been treated as the nexus of Б─°high artБ─² in the Western canon. The NasherБ─≥s focus on these genres is paired with representations of global cultures and people of color, spotlighting how these depictions have been manifestly omitted from art during the last several centuries. Importantly, the collection prioritizes expressions of joy, resilience, and individuality.
Alfredo Jaar, Б─°Lagos 2002Б─² (1991), lightbox with two color transparencies, edition 2/2, 66 x 132 inches. б╘ Alfredo Jaar, image courtesy of the artist and Galerie Lelong & Co., New York
All of the works in Everything Now All At Once are part of the NasherБ─≥s permanent collection, highlighting its accession strategy over the past twenty years with a focus on iconic pieces by artists from historically marginalized backgrounds. From Jeffrey GibsonБ─≥s landmark beaded punching bag series to Amy SheraldБ─≥s brightly backgrounded portraits, visitors can glimpse incredible creativity by dozens of artists in a show that highlights voices that have been drastically underrepresented in the canon.
Ongoing since August 2025, Everything Now All At Once will rotate new pieces into the exhibition next month and continue through November 1 in Durham, North Carolina. 
Nicolas Lambelet Coleman, Б─°We DonБ─≥t Sweat in These ClothesБ─² (2024), oil on canvas, 51 3/16 x 39 3/8 inches. б╘ Nicolas Lambelet Coleman. Photo courtesy of the artist
Barbara Chase-Riboud, Б─°Malcolm X #5Б─² (2003), polished bronze and silk with steel support, 76 1/2 x 29 x 27 inches. б╘ Barbara Chase-Riboud, photo by Peter Paul Geoffrion
Installation view of Б─≤Everything Now All At OnceБ─≥ at the Nasher Museum of Art at Duke University, Durham, North Carolina
Jeffrey Gibson, Б─°I PUT A SPELL ON YOUБ─² (2015), repurposed punching bag, glass beads, artificial sinew, and steel,  40 x 14 x 14 inches. б╘ Jeffrey Gibson, photo by Peter Paul Geoffrion
Ruben Natal-San Miguel, Б─°Priceless (Anthony), Astoria Queens NYC, 7/10/15 77 Degrees 6:40PMБ─² (2015), Kodak Endura fine art lustre-finish medium archival print, edition 1/2, 30 x 30 inches (76.2 ц≈ 76.2 cm). б╘ Ruben Natal-San Miguel
Xaviera Simmons, Б─°Session Six: Kitty HawkБ─² from the project Б─≤Thundersnow Road, North CarolinaБ─≥ (2010), chromogenic print, edition 1/3, 40 1/8 x 50 x 1/8 inches. б╘ Xaviera Simmons, courtesy of the artist and David Castillo, Miami
Installation view of Б─≤Everything Now All At OnceБ─≥ at the Nasher Museum of Art at Duke University, Durham, North Carolina
Wangari Mathenge, Б─°The Ascendants XIV (She is Here)Б─² (2021), oil on canvas, 85 x 64 inches. б╘ Wangari Mathenge, 2021. Courtesy of the artist and Roberts Projects, Los Angeles

schema.headline: Nasher Museum's 'Everything Now All At Once' Celebrates Diversity, Resilience, and Joy

title: Mirei Monticelli's Hand-Woven Banana Fiber Lamps Swell Between Material and Movement
description: Milan-based Filipina designer Mirei Monticelli creates biomorphic lighting fixtures that toe the line between sculpture and utility. Undulating ou
body:
Milan-based Filipina designer Mirei Monticelli creates biomorphic lighting fixtures that toe the line between sculpture and utility. Undulating outward and glowing from within, the artistБ─≥s works feel as if they are alive, quietly dancing wherever they stand or hang.
These gestural, biodegradable structures are crafted with hand-woven Banaca fabric made from Abacц║, a fiber that grows abundantly in MonticelliБ─≥s native Philippines. The artistБ─≥s studio works directly with a community of weavers in the Bicol province at the southeastern end of Luzon, sharing with Colossal, Б─°WeБ─≥ve developed the material together over time, so itБ─≥s not just sourcing, but a relationship.Б─² 

The laborious act of harvesting Abacц║ fiber has long been communal. From gathering the wild plantБ─≥s towering stalks and stripping them layer by layer to sun-drying bundles of knotted thread and hand-weaving the strands into functional textiles, the necessity of human connection has always been part of the process. 
The term BanacaБ─■coined by MonticelliБ─■combines modern elements of design with a heritage technique that has been passed down for centuries. MonticelliБ─≥s contemporary subversion of a material so deeply engrained within Philippine culture further emphasizes the worksБ─≥ metamorphic and dynamic presence. Б─°Human rhythm is what gives the material its character, and itБ─≥s also why every piece feels alive when itБ─≥s lit,Б─² says the artist.
MonticelliБ─≥s practice also incoporates techniques that echo garment construction and fashion. The artist shared that many of her methods are also learned from her mother, a fashion designer. Draping, volume-building, and creating shape are present in MonticelliБ─≥s lamps, underscoring a bodily essence within their surging forms.
Last month, the artist unveiled an installation titled Б─°Pleasure GardenБ─² at Milan Design Week, and often collaborates with interior designers, hospitality partners, and architectural studios to create immersive spaces. Find more from Monticelli on Instagram.

Photo by Juan Padilla
